title: Apple custard Danish
subtitle: Puff pastry parcels filled with crème pâtissière and glazed apple
excerpt: Apple custard Danish: squares of all-butter puff pastry folded into an open parcel, filled with vanilla crème pâtissière and fanned slices of eating apple, brushed with apricot jam and baked until golden. Uses shop-bought puff pastry — most of the work is making the custard.
sourceNotes: Apple custard Danish pastries draw on the wienerbrød tradition brought to Denmark by Austrian bakers in the nineteenth century, which spread across Scandinavia and into British and American baking. The combination of laminated pastry, custard, and fruit topping is widely documented in European domestic baking guides throughout the twentieth century. The simplified version using ready-made puff pastry with crème pâtissière and fresh apple is fully public-domain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the crème pâtissière the day before and refrigerate. Assemble and bake on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, 20 to 25 minutes until pastry is deep gold and apple edges are caramelised

glossaryTerms.definition: To shape a square pastry into a Danish parcel: lay the pastry flat, score a 1.5 cm border around the edge without cutting through, and use a fork to dock the inner square. The border is left undocked so it rises into a frame. Fold each corner inward to the centre, pressing to secure (pinwheel shape), or leave flat with a raised border all around. The inner square holds the custard filling and stays flatter; the border puffs up around it. Chill the shaped pastries for 15 minutes before filling to help the border hold its shape.

content.text: All-butter puff pastry is the essential shortcut here. Cheaper all-fat versions taste greasy rather than buttery. Make the custard the day before and the assembly becomes a 15-minute job.

Whisk the {{egg-yolks}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{cornflour}} together. Heat the {{whole-milk}} until steaming and pour slowly over the egg mixture, whisking constantly. Return to the pan and cook over medium heat, stirring, until thick and bubbling. Cook for 2 more minutes. Stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}. Press cling film onto the surface and refrigerate until cold.
Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Cut the {{puff-pastry}} into 8 squares, roughly 12 × 12 cm. See the
note. Arrange on lined baking trays. Chill for 15 minutes.

Spoon a tablespoon of cold crème pâtissière into the centre of each pastry. Fan 4 to 5 slices of {{apple-eating}} over the custard. Brush the pastry borders with beaten {{eggs}}. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the pastry is deep gold and the apple edges are beginning to caramelise. Warm the {{apricot-jam}} with a splash of water, sieve, and brush over the apples while hot.
